Hi Fokko, 
Thank you so much for your review. I really appreciate your feedback. I didn't figure out how to reply to your request, so I'll just reply to you here. The main reason why I separate it to operator and sensor is that the success of the training job have two stages: successfully kick off a training job, and the training job successfully finishes. The operator tells about the first status, and the sensor tells the latter one. Also, since a training job is hosted at an AWS instance, not the instance that is hosting Airflow, so this way, other operators can set upstream to the operator, rather than the sensor, if they aren't dependent on the model actually being created. Also, by using the sensor, users can set parameters like poke_interval, which makes more sense for a sensor rather than an operator.

isinstance with `int` is too restrictive  (`isinstance(np.int32(5), int)` returns False on most machines)

`isinstance(height, np.integer)` would match all integer types, but I tend to favor the following form as it allows floats with integer value as well:
`(height % 1 == 0)`

If you go the later route, you probably want to set, `self.height = int(height)` below so integer-like floats get converted to actual integers.

I think it's only simple if you already understand how Airy diffraction works.  I'm not sure everyone using GalSim will remember their physics classes that covered this.

I was looking into where we document how to calculate lam_over_diam and it's really only in Airy that there is a concrete annotated example.  (Also demo3 and demo9 do the calculation.)  So I think a similar explanation/example should be added to OpticalPSF and ChromaticOpticalPSF, since this is kind of a confusing thing.  

However, I've kind of grown fond of the idea of letting GalSim do the `lam * 1.e-9 / diam * 206265` calculation for the user given `lam` and `diam` in their natural units (nm and m).  So could we add that as an option in all these places, but keep the `lam_over_diam` option as well for people who want to use different units or something?  Then in ChromaticOpticalPSF, this would also serve as the fiducial wavelength for the `aberrations`.

I do not have any one reference to prove this, rather it's easier to show you. Yay for spotty (T)CSH documentation...

I have included two scripts, one for (T)CSH and one for Bash. The Bash version is just a convenience that mirrors the (T)CSH script. Both scripts accepts one argument, the argument should be `s` (for source) or something else (for execute).

When a (T)CSH script is sourced the correct way to "bubble" out of the script is via `exit`, this will not kill your actual (T)CSH shell. If however the script is being executed, then `exec /bin/true` or `exec /bin/false` should be used (depending on the error code we want). I am not sure in what universe this made sense...

### Untitled.csh ((T)CSH Script)
```csh
./untitled.csh s  # an ok exit for non-nested scripts
./untitled.csh e  # a proper "bubble" out exit
source ./untitled.csh s  # a proper source exit
source ./untitled.csh e  # will kill top level shell
```

#### The Script
```csh
#!/usr/bin/env csh -f

# exit according to user inputs
if ( "${1}" == "s" ) then
    echo '(T)CSH Sourced - running `exit 1`'
    exit 1
else
    # get True value
    if ( -x "/usr/bin/false" ) then
        set FALSE=/usr/bin/false
    else
        set FALSE=/bin/false
    endif

    echo '(T)CSH Executed - running `exec '${FALSE}'`'
    exec ${FALSE}
endif
```

### Untitled.sh (Bash Script)
```bash
./untitled.sh s  # "can only `return' from a function or sourced script" error
./untitled.sh e  # a proper exit
. ./untitled.sh s  # a proper source exit
. ./untitled.sh e  # will kill top level shell
```

#### The Script
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash -f

# exit according to user inputs
if [ "${1}" == "s" ]; then
    echo 'BASH Sourced - running `return 1`'
    return 1
else
    echo 'BASH Executed - running `exit 1`'
    exit 1
fi
```

Actually there reason for this change is due to the lint rules. If you check the lint rules in [tslint.json](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/ui/web/tslint.json#L19) it has the import of `rxjs` and `rxjs/Rx` blacklisted. 

If you go and run lint checks with `ng lint` you will see it listing a lot of `This import is blacklisted, import a submodule instead`.

Apparently this is done to reduce the final bundle size according to this [comment](https://github.com/angular/angular/issues/20349#issuecomment-343638051).

In our case I believe bundle doesn't matter, as long as the WebUI runs on the local machine, but since this is the default accepted behavior, I think we should follow it unless there is a valid reason not to do so.
